name.en: Camino Portugués da Costa (Coastal — Portuguese section)
name.es: Camino Portugués de la Costa (tramo portugués)

description.en: The Portuguese coastal section of the Camino Portugués da Costa, running from Porto along the Atlantic coast through Vila do Conde, Esposende, Viana do Castelo, and Caminha to the border ferry crossing at A Guarda. Approximately 110 km in 5 stages. The Spanish continuation through Oia, Baiona, Vigo, and Redondela (where it merges with the Central route to Santiago) is currently a stub — full Spanish-side data is planned for a future release. The Coastal Way is the second-most-walked Camino Portugués route after the Central, accounting for ~17% of all 2025 Compostelas — nearly tied with Central. Grew from very small numbers in the 2010s to 89,511 pilgrims in 2025.

overview.distanceNote: Sum of 5 stages Porto → Caminha ferry per Gronze.com. The full traditional Coastal route is ~270-280 km Porto → Santiago; this release covers the Portuguese section only (Porto → Caminha ferry → A Guarda). The Spanish continuation through Oia, Baiona, Vigo, and Redondela uses fragmented OSM relations not yet integrated. From A Guarda or Vigo, pilgrims continuing to Santiago can join the Central route at Redondela for the final ~80 km via Pontevedra and Padrón.

name.en: A Guarda, Spain (border ferry crossing from Caminha)

origin.en: The Coastal Way (Caminho da Costa) is the historically older Atlantic-shore variant of the Camino Portugués, walked since at least the 12th century by pilgrims who preferred the seaward route — documented users include Thomas Becket, Archbishop of Canterbury. The Senda Litoral (strict beachfront path) and the slightly inland 'official' Coastal route both run from Porto north along the Portuguese Atlantic coast to the Minho/Miño river border at Caminha, where pilgrims cross to A Guarda in Spain. The Coastal route was officially recognized as a Camino by the Cathedral of Santiago in 2016 and fully marked by 2017; from 2,600 pilgrims in 2016 it has grown to 89,511 in 2025, the fastest-growing major Camino by percentage.

description.en: At Caminha, the Coastal Camino crosses the Minho/Miño river to A Guarda in Spain — the equivalent of the Central route's Ponte Internacional crossing at Tui/Valença. The original municipal ferry (Santa Rita de Cássia) has been out of service since 2020; the current crossing is operated by two private small-boat services — Xacobeo Transfer and Taxi Boat Peregrinos — running in the peak April-October season. The crossing takes about 10 minutes and costs roughly €6 per pilgrim (more for cyclists). Pilgrims who miss the last crossing overnight in Caminha or detour inland ~30 km to cross the Minho at the Tui international bridge.

Unlike the inland Central route, the Coastal Camino is on or near the Atlantic Ocean for most of its length. Pilgrims walk along boardwalks at Esposende, beach paths at Apúlia, harbour-fronts at Viana do Castelo, and cliff trails near Caminha. The constant sound of surf is part of the experience and distinguishes this Camino from any other.

etiquette.en: The Caminha crossing runs limited hours. Peak season (April-October) schedules vary: Xacobeo Transfer typically 07:30-15:30, Taxi Boat Peregrinos typically 07:00-17:00 with departures every 30 minutes. Winter service is sharply reduced to 1-2 morning crossings. Always confirm the current schedule in Caminha. The crossing takes about 10 minutes.
Coastal weather is highly variable. Atlantic storms can roll in suddenly. Boardwalks and beach paths can be slippery in rain.
The route has more Portuguese-only signage than the Central, which has Galician translations from Tui onwards.

associatedLiterature.title: The Camino Portugués: From Lisbon and Porto to Santiago – Central, Coastal and Spiritual Caminos
associatedLiterature.author: Kat Davis, Howard Miller

relevance.en: Cicerone Press, 2nd edition (2023). Covers the Coastal Way alongside the Central and Spiritual variants. The most detailed English-language guide for the Coastal route.

historicalSignificance.en: The Coastal Way is the second-most-walked Camino Portugués and the third-most-walked Camino overall after the Frances and the Portugués Central. In 2025 it had 89,511 walkers (16.9% of all Compostelas), only 11,328 fewer than the Central. The dramatic growth from <3,000 walkers in 2016 to ~90,000 in 2025 makes it the fastest-growing major Camino by percentage in recent years. Despite its popularity, the Coastal route is not separately UNESCO inscribed; the Camino Portugués routes are not currently on the UNESCO World Heritage list (only on the Tentative List as a Portugal-side proposal).

notes.en: Open year-round. Atlantic weather is highly variable. The Caminha ferry has reduced winter schedules. Best months are April-June and September-October.

accommodationNotes.en: Coastal towns have abundant tourism infrastructure (this is a major Portuguese vacation coast). Pensões, hotels, and Airbnb-style rentals are everywhere. Pilgrim albergues are present but less dense than on the Central route.

notes.en: The starting airport for both Camino Portugués variants. Metro Line E (Violeta) connects to central Porto in 30 minutes.

name.en: Caminha ↔ A Guarda crossing (Xacobeo Transfer / Taxi Boat Peregrinos)

notes.en: Small passenger boat across the Minho/Miño river border. The original municipal ferry Santa Rita de Cássia has been out of service since 2020; current crossings are operated by two private small-boat services — Xacobeo Transfer (~07:30-15:30) and Taxi Boat Peregrinos (~07:00-17:00 every 30 minutes) — in peak April-October season. Reduced winter service (1-2 morning crossings). ~€6 per pilgrim, ~10 minute crossing. The signature crossing of the Coastal Camino — pilgrims who miss the last boat overnight in Caminha or detour ~30 km inland to cross at the Tui international bridge.
Comboios de Portugal regional trains run along the Coastal route, allowing pilgrims to start mid-route or skip sections.
